exercises.title: Tokenize with edge cases
exercises.prompt: Write a simple word tokenizer and test it on "Don't run ‚Äî the cats' toys cost $3.50!". List which cases (contractions, possessives, currency, punctuation) a naive split() gets wrong.
exercises.hint: A bare str.split() splits on whitespace only, so punctuation sticks to words. Use a regex to separate words, contractions, and numbers.
exercises.solution:
```python
import re
def tokenize(text):
    return re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\w+'\w+|\w+|[^\w\s]", text.lower())
print(tokenize("Don't run ‚Äî the cats' toys cost $3.50!"))
# ["don't", 'run', '‚Äî', 'the', 'cats', "'", 'toys', 'cost', '$', '3.50', '!']
```

Naive `split()` produces `["don't", 'run', '‚Äî', 'the', "cats'", 'toys', 'cost', '$3.50!']` ‚Äî punctuation glued to words, so `$3.50!` and `cats'` become unique tokens that fragment the vocabulary. Consistent tokenization matters because the model learns per-token statistics; if `don't` and `do n't` both appear, the training signal splits between them.

exercises.title: Stemming vs lemmatization
exercises.prompt: Demonstrate the difference between stemming and lemmatization on the words ['running','ran','better','organization']. Explain when each is the right choice and the failure each can cause.
exercises.hint: Stemming chops suffixes by rules (fast, crude). Lemmatization maps to dictionary root using POS (accurate, slower). 'better'‚Üí'good' needs a lemmatizer + adjective POS.
exercises.solution:
```python
from nltk.stem import PorterStemmer, WordNetLemmatizer
ps=PorterStemmer(); wl=WordNetLemmatizer()
for w in ['running','ran','better','organization']:
    print(w, '| stem:', ps.stem(w), '| lemma:', wl.lemmatize(w, 'v' if w in ('running','ran') else 'a' if w=='better' else 'n'))
# running | stem: run    | lemma: run
# ran     | stem: ran    | lemma: run      (stemmer can't, lemma can)
# better  | stem: better | lemma: good     (needs adjective POS)
# organization | stem: organ | lemma: organization
```

**Stemming** is fast rule-based suffix chopping ‚Äî good enough for search/IR where recall matters and exactness doesn't, but it over-collapses (`organization`‚Üí`organ`) and can't handle irregulars (`ran`‚Üõ`run`). **Lemmatization** returns real dictionary roots using POS context ‚Äî needed when meaning matters (topic modeling, where `organ`‚âÝ`organization` is critical). The classic bug: lemmatizing without passing POS, so verbs default to nouns and `running` stays `running`.

exercises.title: Why POS context matters for lemmas
exercises.prompt: Show that the same surface word lemmatizes differently depending on its part of speech, using a word like 'saw' (verb 'see' vs noun 'saw'). Explain why this couples preprocessing to POS tagging and what breaks if you skip it.
exercises.hint: 'saw' as a verb ‚Üí 'see'; as a noun ‚Üí 'saw'. The lemmatizer needs the POS tag, which means you must POS-tag before lemmatizing.
exercises.solution:
```python
from nltk.stem import WordNetLemmatizer
wl=WordNetLemmatizer()
print(wl.lemmatize('saw', 'v'))   # 'see'  (verb)
print(wl.lemmatize('saw', 'n'))   # 'saw'  (noun)
print(wl.lemmatize('saw'))        # 'saw'  (defaults to noun -> wrong for the verb)
```

The word `saw` is genuinely ambiguous: 'I saw a film' (verb‚Üísee) vs 'a power saw' (noun‚Üísaw). The lemmatizer **cannot resolve this without the POS tag**, so a correct pipeline must **POS-tag first, then lemmatize** with that tag. Skip it and `WordNetLemmatizer` defaults every word to noun ‚Äî so all your verbs stay in surface form, `running` never becomes `run`, and downstream features (counts, topic clusters) silently fragment. This is why lesson 07 (POS tagging) is a prerequisite for clean preprocessing, not an afterthought.
